transcript:
That word "whose" sitting huge and pale in the center, with everything else — hundreds of little phrases — drifting and flickering around it, never settling. Nothing here resolves. It just keeps breathing.

Look at the list the artist pulls fragments from, called claimable thoughts, near the top of the script. Lines like "was this mine," "I have thought this," "or will think this" — sitting right next to "unclaimed" and "claimed" as separate, competing entries. The piece literally can't decide which bucket a thought belongs in, so it draws from both at random, forever.

And notice the animations doing the dissolving — they're named corrupt-a, corrupt-b, corrupt-c, corrupt-d in the style section. Each phrase gets assigned one of four corruption patterns, so the moment of a thought becoming "clear" is coded, quite literally, as corruption. Clarity and decay are the same function here.

So the vertigo isn't a metaphor layered on top — it's structural. Every phrase you manage to read sharply is, by the code's own naming, already corrupting.
